items.subtopic: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities | PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Understanding Peripheral Artery Disease: Causes and Risk Factors
items.content: Peripheral artery disease (PAD) is primarily caused by atherosclerosis in individuals over 40, with other causes including thrombosis, embolism, vasculitis, fibromuscular dysplasia, entrapment, cystic adventitial disease, and trauma. PAD prevalence increases with age, particularly in the sixth and seventh decades, and is associated with certain risk factors.
keyPoints: Atherosclerosis is the leading cause of PAD in patients >40 years old.
Prevalence of PAD is higher in black individuals compared to non-Hispanic whites and those of lower socioeconomic status.
Increased risk of PAD in cigarette smokers, persons with diabetes mellitus, hypercholesterolemia, elevated lipoprotein(a), hypertension, or renal insufficiency.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Arterial Diseases of the Extremities | PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities | PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Clinical Presentation and Symptomatology of PAD
items.content: PAD often presents with intermittent claudication, characterized by pain or discomfort in muscles during exercise that is relieved by rest. Symptoms are more common in the lower extremities due to higher incidence of obstructive lesions. Severe cases may lead to chronic limb-threatening ischemia with symptoms like rest pain and coldness.
keyPoints: Intermittent claudication occurs in 50% or fewer patients with PAD.
Symptoms are more common in the lower extremities than upper due to obstructive lesions.
Chronic limb-threatening ischemia presents as rest pain, coldness, and may lead to ulcers or gangrene.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Arterial Diseases of the Extremities | PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities | PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)
items.title: Vascular Examination in PAD: Key Physical Findings
items.content: A comprehensive vascular examination for PAD includes palpation of peripheral pulses, auscultation for bruits, and inspection for physical signs. Decreased or absent pulses, presence of bruits, muscle atrophy, hair loss, thickened nails, shiny skin, reduced temperature, pallor, cyanosis, ulcers, gangrene, and ischemic neuropathy are significant findings.
keyPoints: Palpation includes femoral, popliteal, dorsalis pedis, and posterior tibial arteries.
Physical signs of PAD include decreased pulses, bruits, muscle atrophy, hair loss, thickened nails, shiny skin, reduced temperature, pallor or cyanosis.
Chronic limb-threatening ischemia may present with ulcers, gangrene, peripheral edema, numbness, and hyporeflexia.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Arterial Diseases of the Extremities | PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Understanding the Ankle-Brachial Index (ABI) for PAD Diagnosis
items.content: The ABI is a crucial noninvasive test used to diagnose Peripheral Artery Disease (PAD). It involves measuring systolic blood pressure at the ankle and arm, with normal values ranging from 1.00 to 1.40. An ABI of ≤0.90 indicates PAD, while values >1.40 suggest noncompressible arteries due to vascular calcification.
keyPoints: Normal ABI: 1.00–1.40
Borderline ABI: 0.91–0.99
Abnormal ABI (indicative of PAD): ≤0.90
Noncompressible arteries (e.g., in diabetes or chronic kidney disease): ABI >1.40
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Role of Toe-Brachial Index (TBI) when ABI is Inconclusive
items.content: In cases where the ABI cannot be accurately assessed due to vascular calcification, the TBI serves as an alternative diagnostic tool. A TBI value of ≤0.70 is considered abnormal and indicative of PAD.
keyPoints: TBI used when ABI is inconclusive due to vascular calcification
Abnormal TBI (indicative of PAD): ≤0.70
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Prognosis and Mortality in Patients with PAD
items.content: Patients with Peripheral Artery Disease have a significant risk of cardiovascular mortality, with a 15–25% 5-year mortality rate. The presence of coexisting coronary artery disease further influences prognosis, with high rates of progression to chronic limb-threatening ischemia.
keyPoints: 5-year mortality rate for PAD patients: 15–25%
Increased risk of cardiovascular death: two- to fourfold
Progression to chronic limb-threatening ischemia in ~11% of symptomatic PAD patients
Amputation rate without revascularization in chronic limb-threatening ischemia: ~25% within 1 year
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Management of Cardiovascular Risk Factors in PAD
items.content: Patients with Peripheral Artery Disease (PAD) should receive therapies aimed at reducing cardiovascular events and improving limb symptoms. This includes risk factor modification, antithrombotic therapy, smoking cessation, blood pressure control, and lipid management.
keyPoints: Discontinuing cigarette smoking is crucial for PAD patients.
Antihypertensive agents like ACE inhibitors and ARBs may reduce cardiovascular events in symptomatic PAD.
$\beta$-Adrenergic blockers do not worsen claudication and can treat hypertension, especially with coexistent CAD.
High-intensity statin treatment is recommended to achieve a 50% or greater reduction in LDL cholesterol for patients with atherosclerotic disorders including PAD.
GLP-1 agonists and SGLT2 inhibitors have beneficial cardiovascular effects and may reduce amputation risk in diabetic patients with PAD.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Peripheral Artery Disease (part 1)

items.title: Antiplatelet Therapy in PAD
items.content: Platelet inhibitors, including aspirin and clopidogrel, are recommended for reducing adverse cardiovascular events in symptomatic PAD. The benefit of dual antiplatelet therapy is uncertain, while vorapaxar decreases the risk of adverse cardiovascular events but increases moderate bleeding.
keyPoints: Aspirin and ADP antagonist clopidogrel reduce the risk of adverse cardiovascular events in patients with symptomatic PAD.
Outcomes with ticagrelor are similar to those with clopidogrel.
Vorapaxar decreases the risk of acute limb ischemia and peripheral revascularization but is associated with increased moderate bleeding.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Peripheral Artery Disease (part 1)

items.title: Exercise Training for Claudication in PAD
items.content: Supervised exercise training programs are effective in prolonging walking distance for patients with claudication, often matching or exceeding the benefits of revascularization procedures.
keyPoints: Supervised exercise training programs should consist of 30- to 45-min sessions at least three times per week for 12 weeks.
The beneficial effect on walking performance in patients with claudication is similar to or greater than that realized after a revascularization procedure.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Peripheral Artery Disease (part 1)

items.title: Indications for Revascularization in PAD
items.content: Revascularization is indicated for patients with chronic limb-threatening ischemia to relieve pain and prevent limb loss. It's also recommended for those with disabling, progressive, or severe symptoms of intermittent claudication despite medical therapy to improve walking distance and functional capacity.
keyPoints: Indicated for chronic limb-threatening ischemia
Also indicated for severe intermittent claudication unresponsive to medical therapy
Improves walking distance more when combined with supervised exercise
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Peripheral Artery Disease (part 2) | REVASCULARIZATION

items.title: Preoperative Cardiac Risk Assessment in PAD Patients
items.content: Patients undergoing revascularization for PAD should undergo preoperative cardiac risk assessment to identify those at increased risk of adverse cardiac events. This is particularly important for patients with angina, prior myocardial infarction, heart failure, diabetes, or renal insufficiency.
keyPoints: Cardiac risk assessment identifies high-risk individuals
Includes stress testing and imaging for further stratification
Close supervision needed for those with abnormal test results
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Peripheral Artery Disease (part 2) | REVASCULARIZATION

items.title: Imaging Techniques for Revascularization Planning in PAD
items.content: MRA, CTA, or conventional angiography should be performed to assess vascular anatomy in patients being considered for revascularization. These imaging techniques help determine the appropriate intervention strategy based on the location and extent of arterial occlusions.
keyPoints: Use MRA, CTA, or conventional angiography for anatomical assessment
Helps decide between endovascular, open surgical, or hybrid strategies
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Peripheral Artery Disease (part 2) | REVASCULARIZATION

items.title: Understanding Fibromuscular Dysplasia: Diagnosis and Management
items.content: Fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD) is a vascular disorder affecting medium to small arteries, often leading to stenosis or aneurysms. It predominantly affects females and involves renal and carotid/vertebral arteries but can also affect extremity vessels like the iliac and subclavian arteries. The angiographic classification includes multifocal (string of beads appearance) and focal types (unifocal <1 cm, tubular ≥1 cm stenoses). Management includes aspirin to reduce thrombosis risk and PTA or surgical reconstruction for severe symptoms.
keyPoints: FMD affects medium to small arteries, often renal and carotid/vertebral but can involve extremity vessels like iliac and subclavian.
Angiographic classification: multifocal (string of beads) and focal (unifocal <1 cm, tubular ≥1 cm stenoses).
Aspirin is recommended to reduce thrombosis risk; PTA and surgical reconstruction for severe symptoms.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · FIBROMUSCULAR DYSPLASIA | THROMBOANGIITIS OBLITERANS

items.title: Thromboangiitis Obliterans: Clinical Features and Management
items.content: Thromboangiitis obliterans, or Buerger's disease, is an inflammatory occlusive disorder affecting small to medium arteries in distal extremities. It primarily affects men under 40, with a higher prevalence among Asians and Eastern Europeans. The condition is strongly linked to cigarette smoking. Clinical features include claudication, Raynaud phenomenon, and migratory superficial vein thrombophlebitis. Diagnosis involves imaging like MRA or CTA showing smooth, tapering segmental lesions. Management focuses on tobacco cessation; arterial bypass may be used in severe cases.
keyPoints: Affects small to medium arteries in distal extremities, primarily men <40 years old.
Strongly linked to cigarette smoking.
Clinical features: claudication, Raynaud phenomenon, migratory superficial vein thrombophlebitis.
Diagnosis via MRA or CTA showing smooth, tapering segmental lesions.
Management focuses on tobacco cessation; arterial bypass in severe cases.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · FIBROMUSCULAR DYSPLASIA | THROMBOANGIITIS OBLITERANS

items.title: Causes of Acute Limb Ischemia
items.content: Acute limb ischemia is primarily caused by arterial occlusion, which can result from embolism, thrombus in situ, arterial dissection, or trauma. The most common sources of arterial emboli include the heart and aorta, with cardiac disorders such as atrial fibrillation and acute myocardial infarction being significant contributors.
keyPoints: Principal causes: embolism, thrombus in situ, arterial dissection, trauma
Common sources of arterial emboli: heart, aorta, large arteries

Cardiac disorders causing thromboembolism: atrial fibrillation, acute myocardial infarction, ventricular aneurysm, cardiomyopathy, infectious and marantic endocarditis, prosthetic heart valves, atrial myxoma
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · OTHER VASCULITIDES | ACUTE LIMB ISCHEMIA

items.title: Sites of Embolus Lodgment in Acute Limb Ischemia
items.content: Arterial emboli tend to lodge at vessel bifurcations due to the decrease in vessel caliber. In the lower extremities, emboli most frequently lodge in the femoral artery, followed by the iliac artery, aorta, and popliteal and tibioperoneal arteries.
keyPoints: Emboli lodge at vessel bifurcations
Common sites of embolus lodgment: femoral artery (most frequent), iliac artery, aorta, popliteal and tibioperoneal arteries
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · OTHER VASCULITIDES | ACUTE LIMB ISCHEMIA

items.title: Recognizing and Managing Acute Arterial Occlusion
items.content: Acute arterial occlusion presents with severe pain, paresthesia, numbness, and coldness within 1 hour. In cases of adequate collateral circulation, symptoms are less severe, characterized by a decrease in walking distance before claudication or modest pain and paresthesia. Diagnosis is typically clinical but confirmed via imaging such as duplex ultrasound, MRA, CTA, or arteriography.
keyPoints: Symptoms develop within 1 hour of occlusion
Physical findings include loss of pulses, cyanosis/pallor, mottling, decreased skin temperature, muscle stiffening, loss of sensation, weakness, absent deep tendon reflexes
Adequate collateral circulation may present with less severe symptoms
Diagnosis confirmed by duplex ultrasound, MRA, CTA, or arteriography
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · CLINICAL FEATURES | Acute Limb Ischemia

items.title: Intervention Strategies for Acute Limb Ischemia
items.content: Immediate anticoagulation with intravenous heparin is crucial to prevent clot propagation. In severe ischemia, reperfusion interventions such as catheter-directed thrombolysis/thrombectomy, surgical thromboembolectomy, or arterial bypass are indicated. Thrombolytic therapy is effective for recent occlusions in atherosclerotic vessels and when surgery is contraindicated. Surgical revascularization is preferred within 24 hours to prevent limb loss.
keyPoints: Anticoagulate with intravenous heparin immediately
Interventions include catheter-directed thrombolysis/thrombectomy, surgical thromboembolectomy, arterial bypass
Thrombolytic therapy effective for recent occlusions in atherosclerotic vessels
Surgical revascularization preferred within 24 hours to prevent limb loss
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · CLINICAL FEATURES | Acute Limb Ischemia

items.title: Atheroembolism: Causes and Management of Limb Ischemia
items.content: Atheroembolism results from emboli composed of fibrin, platelets, and cholesterol debris originating from atherosclerotic lesions or aneurysms. These emboli lodge in small vessels, causing limb ischemia without occluding large vessels, thus preserving distal pulses. Symptoms include acute pain, tenderness, and potential digital necrosis leading to 'blue toe' syndrome. Management is challenging; local foot care or amputation may be necessary for necrotic areas, while antiplatelet drugs and statins improve cardiovascular outcomes but do not prevent recurrence.
keyPoints: Atheroembolism causes limb ischemia by emboli lodging in small vessels of muscle and skin.
Distal pulses usually remain palpable despite atheroembolic events.
Symptoms include acute pain, tenderness, 'blue toe' syndrome, digital necrosis, and gangrene.
Management includes local foot care or amputation for necrotic areas; analgesics for pain relief.
Neither surgical revascularization nor thrombolytic therapy is typically helpful due to emboli's multiplicity and distal location.
Antiplatelet drugs and statins improve cardiovascular outcomes but do not prevent recurrent atheroembolism.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · ATHEROEMBOLISM | THORACIC OUTLET COMPRESSION SYNDROME

items.title: Thoracic Outlet Compression Syndrome: Etiology and Clinical Presentation
items.content: Thoracic outlet compression syndrome arises from neurovascular bundle compression at the thoracic outlet, often due to cervical ribs or muscle abnormalities. It is categorized into arterial, venous, and neurogenic forms based on affected structures. Neurogenic form presents with shoulder/arm pain, weakness, and paresthesias. Arterial compression leads to claudication, Raynaud phenomenon, ischemic tissue loss, and gangrene. Venous compression can cause thrombosis of the subclavian and axillary veins, known as Paget-Schroetter syndrome.
keyPoints: Thoracic outlet compression syndrome results from neurovascular bundle compression at the thoracic outlet.
Causes include cervical ribs, scalenus anticus abnormalities, clavicle proximity to first rib, or abnormal pectoralis minor insertion.
Divided into arterial, venous, and neurogenic forms based on affected structures.
Neurogenic form symptoms: shoulder/arm pain, weakness, paresthesias.
Arterial compression symptoms: claudication, Raynaud phenomenon, ischemic tissue loss, gangrene.
Venous compression can cause thrombosis of subclavian and axillary veins (Paget-Schroetter syndrome).
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · ATHEROEMBOLISM | THORACIC OUTLET COMPRESSION SYNDROME

items.title: Diagnosing Arterial Thoracic Outlet Compression Syndrome with Provocative Maneuvers
items.content: Arterial thoracic outlet compression syndrome may present with normal examination findings unless specific provocative maneuvers are performed. These maneuvers can elicit symptoms, a subclavian artery bruit, and diminished arm pulses, aiding in diagnosis.
keyPoints: Provocative maneuvers include abduction and external rotation test (arm abducted $90^{	ext{o}}$), scalene maneuver, costoclavicular maneuver, and hyperabduction maneuver (raising the arm $180^{	ext{o}}$).
Cervical ribs can be indicated by a chest x-ray.
Imaging techniques such as duplex ultrasonography, CTA, MRA, and contrast angiography during provocative maneuvers can demonstrate thoracic outlet compression of the subclavian artery.
Neurophysiologic tests may show abnormalities if the brachial plexus is involved, but normal results do not exclude neurogenic thoracic outlet syndrome due to low sensitivity.
Conservative management includes avoiding positions that cause symptoms and performing shoulder girdle exercises; surgical intervention may be necessary for symptom relief or treatment of ischemia.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Arterial Thoracic Outlet Compression Syndrome | POPLITEAL ARTERY ENTRAPMENT

items.title: Identifying and Treating Popliteal Artery Entrapment in Young Athletes
items.content: Popliteal artery entrapment is often seen in young athletes, where the gastrocnemius or popliteus muscle compresses the popliteal artery. This condition can lead to intermittent claudication and may result in thrombosis, embolism, or aneurysm.
keyPoints: The pulse examination might be normal unless provocative maneuvers like ankle dorsiflexion and plantar flexion are performed.
Diagnosis is confirmed using duplex ultrasound, CTA, MRA, or conventional angiography.
Treatment involves surgical release of the popliteal artery or vascular reconstruction.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Arterial Thoracic Outlet Compression Syndrome | POPLITEAL ARTERY ENTRAPMENT

items.title: Indications for Repair of Popliteal Artery Aneurysm
items.content: Popliteal artery aneurysms are the most common peripheral artery aneurysms and often present with limb ischemia due to thrombosis or embolism. They can be detected by palpation and confirmed via duplex ultrasonography.
keyPoints: Most common peripheral artery aneurysm
Approximately 50% are bilateral
Commonly associated with aortic aneurysms
Symptomatic presentation often due to limb ischemia from thrombosis or embolism
Repair indicated for symptomatic cases or when diameter exceeds 2–3 cm
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · POPLITEAL ARTERY ANEURYSM | ARTERIOVENOUS FISTULA

items.title: Diagnosis and Management of Arteriovenous Fistula
items.content: Arteriovenous fistulas can be congenital or acquired, with clinical features including a pulsatile mass, thrill, bruit, and signs of chronic venous insufficiency. Diagnosis is often evident from physical examination.
keyPoints: Congenital arteriovenous fistulas may be associated with birthmarks
Acquired arteriovenous fistulas can result from hemodialysis access or penetrating injuries
Physical exam findings include pulsatile mass, thrill, and bruit
Chronic venous insufficiency signs due to high venous pressure
Diagnosis confirmed by duplex ultrasonography, CTA, or angiography
Management includes surgery, radiotherapy, embolization, or conservative treatment with elastic support hose
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · POPLITEAL ARTERY ANEURYSM | ARTERIOVENOUS FISTULA

items.title: Characteristics of Raynaud Phenomenon
items.content: Raynaud phenomenon involves episodic digital ischemia, characterized by a triphasic color change in digits following cold exposure or emotional stress. The phases include blanching (ischemic phase), cyanosis, and rubor (reactive hyperemia). Physical examination is often normal between episodes.
keyPoints: Episodic digital ischemia with triphasic color changes: pallor, cyanosis, and rubor
Common triggers include cold exposure and emotional stress
Physical exam usually normal; radial, ulnar, and pedal pulses are normal
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · RAYNAUD PHENOMENON (part 1)

items.title: Primary vs. Secondary Raynaud Phenomenon
items.content: Raynaud phenomenon is categorized as primary or secondary. Primary, idiopathic form affects over 50% of patients and is more common in women aged 20-40 years. Secondary form is associated with diseases like systemic sclerosis, SLE, dermatomyositis, polymyositis, rheumatoid arthritis, atherosclerosis, thromboangiitis obliterans, thoracic outlet syndrome, and primary pulmonary hypertension.
keyPoints: Primary Raynaud phenomenon: idiopathic, affects >50% of patients
More common in women aged 20-40 years
Secondary causes include systemic sclerosis (80–90%), SLE (~20%), dermatomyositis/polymyositis (~30%)
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · RAYNAUD PHENOMENON (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Course and Prognosis of Primary Raynaud Phenomenon
items.content: Primary Raynaud phenomenon generally has milder manifestations. Approximately 15% of patients experience spontaneous improvement, while about 30% see progression. Less than 1% may lose a digit due to the condition.
keyPoints: Milder clinical manifestations in primary form
~15% of patients experience spontaneous improvement
<1% risk of losing a digit
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · RAYNAUD PHENOMENON (part 1)

items.title: Identify and Manage Secondary Causes of Raynaud Phenomenon
items.content: Raynaud phenomenon can be associated with various blood dyscrasias such as cold agglutinins, cryoglobulinemia, cryofibrinogenemia, myeloproliferative disorders, and Waldenström macroglobulinemia. Occupational exposures like vibrating hand tools or electric shock injuries may also precipitate the condition. Additionally, certain drugs including ergot preparations, methysergide, $ \beta $-adrenergic receptor antagonists, bleomycin, vinblastine, cisplatin, and gemcitabine can cause Raynaud phenomenon.
keyPoints: Cold agglutinins, cryoglobulinemia, cryofibrinogenemia may lead to Raynaud phenomenon
Consider myeloproliferative disorders and Waldenström macroglobulinemia in initial evaluation
Occupational exposure: vibrating hand tools, electric shock injuries
Drugs implicated: ergot preparations, methysergide, $ \beta $-adrenergic receptor antagonists, bleomycin, vinblastine, cisplatin, gemcitabine
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · RAYNAUD PHENOMENON (part 2) | Raynaud Phenomenon

items.title: Treatment Strategies for Severe Raynaud Phenomenon
items.content: For patients with severe Raynaud phenomenon, drug treatment is recommended. Dihydropyridine calcium channel antagonists like nifedipine and amlodipine are effective in reducing frequency and severity of episodes. Prazosin, doxazosin, and terazosin can also be beneficial. Phosphodiesterase type 5 inhibitors such as sildenafil, tadalafil, and vardenafil may improve symptoms in secondary Raynaud phenomenon associated with systemic sclerosis. Topical nitroglycerin preparations have shown efficacy. Digital sympathectomy or botulinum toxin injections into perivascular tissue can be considered for patients unresponsive to medical therapy.
keyPoints: Reserve drug treatment for severe cases
Dihydropyridine calcium channel antagonists: nifedipine, amlodipine
Prazosin, doxazosin, terazosin may be effective
Phosphodiesterase type 5 inhibitors: sildenafil, tadalafil, vardenafil for secondary Raynaud phenomenon
Topical nitroglycerin preparations are effective
Consider digital sympathectomy or botulinum toxin injections for refractory cases
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · RAYNAUD PHENOMENON (part 2) | Raynaud Phenomenon

items.title: Distinguishing Acrocyanosis from Raynaud's Phenomenon
items.content: Acrocyanosis is characterized by persistent cyanosis of the hands and feet, primarily affecting women under 30 years. Unlike Raynaud's phenomenon, acrocyanosis does not exhibit episodic discoloration or blanching, with normal pulses confirming no arterial occlusive disease.
keyPoints: Persistent cyanosis, more common in women <30 years
Normal pulses and absence of trophic changes
Distinguishing feature: persistent rather than episodic, extends proximally without blanching
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · ACROCYANOSIS | LIVEDO RETICULARIS | PERNIO (CHILBLAINS)

items.title: Management of Benign Livedo Reticularis
items.content: Benign livedo reticularis, more common in women during their third decade, presents with symmetric mottling that improves with warmth. Patients are usually asymptomatic and seek care for cosmetic reasons; reassurance and avoidance of cold exposure suffice.
keyPoints: Symmetric mottling improving with warming
Most common in women, onset in the third decade
No pharmacologic treatment needed
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · ACROCYANOSIS | LIVEDO RETICULARIS | PERNIO (CHILBLAINS)

items.subtopic: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities | PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: Which is the leading cause of peripheral artery disease in patients older than 40 years?
items.explanation: Atherosclerosis is the leading cause of PAD in patients >40 years old.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Arterial Diseases of the Extremities | PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities | PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: In which arteries are atherosclerotic lesions most commonly found in symptomatic patients?
items.explanation: The primary sites of involvement are the femoral and popliteal arteries (80–90% of patients).
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Arterial Diseases of the Extremities | PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities | PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: What is the most typical symptom of peripheral artery disease?
items.explanation: The most typical symptom is intermittent claudication.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Arterial Diseases of the Extremities | PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities | PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)
items.question: Which group has a higher prevalence of peripheral artery disease?
items.explanation: Prevalence of PAD is greater in persons identified as black than non-Hispanic white.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Arterial Diseases of the Extremities | PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: What is the normal range for the ankle-brachial index (ABI) in individuals without peripheral artery disease?
items.explanation: The normal ABI range is 1.00–1.40.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: Which ABI value indicates noncompressible arteries often due to vascular calcification?
items.explanation: An ABI >1.40 indicates noncompressible arteries.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: What is considered an abnormal toe-brachial index (TBI) value?
items.explanation: A TBI of ≤0.70 is considered abnormal.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: Which noninvasive test can be used to detect PAD when ABI cannot be accurately assessed due to vascular calcification?
items.explanation: The toe-brachial index (TBI) is used when ABI cannot be accurately assessed.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following medications is recommended for reducing cardiovascular events in patients with symptomatic Peripheral Artery Disease (PAD)?
items.explanation: Angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors may reduce the risk of cardiovascular events in patients with symptomatic PAD.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Peripheral Artery Disease (part 1)

items.question: What is a recommended treatment to improve walking distance in patients with claudication due to PAD?
items.explanation: Supervised exercise training programs for 30- to 45-min sessions, at least three times per week for 12 weeks, prolong walking distance.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Peripheral Artery Disease (part 1)

items.question: Which class of drugs has not been shown to be effective in treating PAD due to its mechanism during exercise?
items.explanation: Vasodilators, including alpha-adrenergic blocking agents, have not been shown to be effective in patients with PAD.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Peripheral Artery Disease (part 1)

items.question: What is the role of statins and PCSK9 inhibitors in the management of PAD?
options: They increase the risk of amputation
They are associated with decreased risk of adverse limb events
They have no effect on cardiovascular outcomes
items.explanation: Statins and PCSK9 inhibitors are associated with a decreased risk of adverse limb events, including amputation, in patients with PAD.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Peripheral Artery Disease (part 1)

items.question: Which medication is a phosphodiesterase-3 inhibitor that increases claudication distance by 40–60%?
items.explanation: Cilostazol is a phosphodiesterase-3 inhibitor with vasodilator and antiplatelet properties that increases claudication distance.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Peripheral Artery Disease (part 2) | REVASCULARIZATION

items.question: Which of the following therapies has not been confirmed to be effective in clinical trials for intermittent claudication?
items.explanation: Enthusiasm for therapy with angiogenic growth factors abated due to lack of improvement in symptoms or outcomes.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Peripheral Artery Disease (part 2) | REVASCULARIZATION

items.question: What is the primary purpose of revascularization procedures in patients with chronic limb-threatening ischemia?
options: To relieve pain and prevent limb loss
items.explanation: Revascularization is indicated to relieve pain and prevent limb loss in patients with chronic limb-threatening ischemia.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Peripheral Artery Disease (part 2) | REVASCULARIZATION

items.question: Which imaging modality should be performed to assess vascular anatomy before revascularization?
items.explanation: MRA, CTA, or conventional angiography are recommended for assessing vascular anatomy in patients considered for revascularization.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Peripheral Artery Disease (part 2) | REVASCULARIZATION

items.question: Which arteries are most commonly affected by fibromuscular dysplasia (FMD)?
items.explanation: FMD most commonly involves the renal and carotid/vertebral arteries.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · FIBROMUSCULAR DYSPLASIA | THROMBOANGIITIS OBLITERANS

items.question: What is a characteristic angiographic appearance of multifocal fibromuscular dysplasia?
items.explanation: The multifocal type of FMD is characterized by a 'string of beads' appearance.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · FIBROMUSCULAR DYSPLASIA | THROMBOANGIITIS OBLITERANS

items.question: Which treatment is recommended to reduce the risk of thrombosis in vessels affected by FMD?
items.explanation: Aspirin is recommended to reduce the risk of thrombosis in vessels affected by FMD.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · FIBROMUSCULAR DYSPLASIA | THROMBOANGIITIS OBLITERANS

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a common source of arterial emboli in acute limb ischemia?
items.explanation: Gastrocnemius muscle entrapment is a less common cause of arterial occlusion, not an embolic source.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · OTHER VASCULITIDES | ACUTE LIMB ISCHEMIA

items.question: Where do arterial emboli most frequently lodge in the lower extremities?
items.explanation: Arterial emboli most frequently lodge in the femoral artery.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · OTHER VASCULITIDES | ACUTE LIMB ISCHEMIA

items.question: Which condition is associated with acute arterial thrombosis due to increased blood viscosity?
items.explanation: Polycythemia and hypercoagulable disorders are associated with acute arterial thrombosis.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · OTHER VASCULITIDES | ACUTE LIMB ISCHEMIA

items.question: Which symptom is most likely to develop within 1 hour of an acute arterial occlusion?
options: Loss of pulses distal to the occlusion
Abrupt decrease in walking distance before claudication
items.explanation: Severe pain, paresthesia, numbness, and coldness develop within 1 hour; loss of pulses is a key early physical finding.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · CLINICAL FEATURES | Acute Limb Ischemia

items.question: What diagnostic tool is usually used to confirm the diagnosis of acute limb ischemia?
items.explanation: The clinical evaluation includes Doppler assessment, and while other tools like MRA, CTA, or catheter-based arteriography are used to confirm diagnosis, the initial clinical presentation is often apparent.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · CLINICAL FEATURES | Acute Limb Ischemia

items.question: In which scenario is intraarterial thrombolytic therapy most effective?
options: When smaller distal vessels are occluded
When a large proximal vessel is occluded
When the patient's overall condition contraindicates surgical intervention
items.explanation: Thrombolytic therapy is indicated when smaller distal vessels are occluded, preventing surgical access.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · CLINICAL FEATURES | Acute Limb Ischemia

items.question: Which of the following symptoms is most characteristic of atheroembolism?
options: Acute pain and tenderness at the site of embolization.
items.explanation: Atheroembolism typically presents with acute pain and tenderness at the site of embolization, while distal pulses usually remain palpable.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · ATHEROEMBOLISM | THORACIC OUTLET COMPRESSION SYNDROME

items.question: Which treatment is generally NOT helpful for ischemia resulting from atheroemboli?
items.explanation: Ischemia from atheroemboli is difficult to treat with surgical revascularization or thrombolytic therapy due to the nature of the emboli.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · ATHEROEMBOLISM | THORACIC OUTLET COMPRESSION SYNDROME

items.question: What is a potential consequence of digital vascular occlusion in atheroembolism?
items.explanation: Digital vascular occlusion in atheroembolism can lead to ischemia and the development of 'blue toe' syndrome.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · ATHEROEMBOLISM | THORACIC OUTLET COMPRESSION SYNDROME

items.subtopic: Arterial Thoracic Outlet Compression Syndrome | POPLITEAL ARTERY ENTRAPMENT
items.question: Which maneuver is used to diagnose arterial thoracic outlet compression syndrome by diminishing arm pulses?
items.explanation: The abduction and external rotation test is used to diminish arm pulses in diagnosing arterial thoracic outlet compression syndrome.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Arterial Thoracic Outlet Compression Syndrome | POPLITEAL ARTERY ENTRAPMENT

items.subtopic: Arterial Thoracic Outlet Compression Syndrome | POPLITEAL ARTERY ENTRAPMENT
items.question: What imaging modality can demonstrate thoracic outlet compression of the subclavian artery during provocative maneuvers?
items.explanation: Duplex ultrasonography can demonstrate thoracic outlet compression of the subclavian artery during provocative maneuvers.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Arterial Thoracic Outlet Compression Syndrome | POPLITEAL ARTERY ENTRAPMENT

items.subtopic: Arterial Thoracic Outlet Compression Syndrome | POPLITEAL ARTERY ENTRAPMENT
items.question: Which conservative management strategy is recommended for patients with arterial thoracic outlet compression syndrome?
options: Avoid positions causing symptoms and perform shoulder girdle exercises
items.explanation: Patients are advised to avoid positions that cause symptoms and may benefit from shoulder girdle exercises.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Arterial Thoracic Outlet Compression Syndrome | POPLITEAL ARTERY ENTRAPMENT

items.question: Which diagnostic method can confirm a popliteal artery aneurysm?
items.explanation: Popliteal artery aneurysms can be detected by palpation and confirmed by duplex ultrasonography.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · POPLITEAL ARTERY ANEURYSM | ARTERIOVENOUS FISTULA

items.question: What is the most common clinical presentation of a popliteal artery aneurysm?
items.explanation: The most common clinical presentation of a popliteal artery aneurysm is limb ischemia secondary to thrombosis or embolism.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · POPLITEAL ARTERY ANEURYSM | ARTERIOVENOUS FISTULA

items.question: Which condition may result from rupture of an arterial aneurysm?
items.explanation: An uncommon cause of arteriovenous fistula is rupture of an arterial aneurysm into a vein.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · POPLITEAL ARTERY ANEURYSM | ARTERIOVENOUS FISTULA

items.question: Which phase of Raynaud phenomenon is characterized by a sensation of cold or numbness in the digits?
items.explanation: The sensation of cold or numbness accompanies the phases of pallor and cyanosis, which are part of the blanching or ischemic phase.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · RAYNAUD PHENOMENON (part 1)

items.question: What is a common diagnostic finding in patients with primary Raynaud phenomenon?
options: Normal superficial capillaries on nailfold capillaroscopy
items.explanation: Nailfold capillaroscopy in primary Raynaud phenomenon reveals normal superficial capillaries, appearing as regularly spaced hairpin loops.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · RAYNAUD PHENOMENON (part 1)

items.question: Which group of patients is more likely to have a milder form of Raynaud phenomenon?
items.explanation: Patients with primary Raynaud disease generally have milder clinical manifestations compared to those with secondary causes.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · RAYNAUD PHENOMENON (part 1)

items.question: What percentage of patients with systemic sclerosis present with Raynaud phenomenon?
items.explanation: Raynaud phenomenon occurs in 80–90% of patients with systemic sclerosis.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · RAYNAUD PHENOMENON (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following conditions is associated with cold-induced precipitation of plasma proteins in patients with Raynaud phenomenon?
options: Vibration from hand tools like chain saws
Drugs such as ergot preparations and methysergide
items.explanation: Cold agglutinins, cryoglobulinemia, or cryofibrinogenemia are associated with cold-induced precipitation of plasma proteins.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · RAYNAUD PHENOMENON (part 2) | Raynaud Phenomenon

items.question: Which group of drugs is implicated in causing Raynaud phenomenon?
items.explanation: $\beta$-adrenergic receptor antagonists and ergot preparations are among the drugs implicated in causing Raynaud phenomenon.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · RAYNAUD PHENOMENON (part 2) | Raynaud Phenomenon

items.question: Which treatment is considered for patients with severe Raynaud phenomenon unresponsive to medical therapy?
options: Avoidance of cold exposure and wearing warm clothing
items.explanation: Digital sympathectomy is helpful for patients unresponsive to medical therapy.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · RAYNAUD PHENOMENON (part 2) | Raynaud Phenomenon

items.question: Which of the following is a distinguishing feature between acrocyanosis and Raynaud phenomenon?
options: Acrocyanosis is episodic while Raynaud phenomenon is persistent.
Blanching occurs in acrocyanosis but not in Raynaud phenomenon.
Acrocyanosis involves normal pulses, unlike Raynaud phenomenon.
The discoloration in acrocyanosis extends proximally from the digits and does not blanch.
items.explanation: Acrocyanosis is persistent, not episodic like Raynaud phenomenon, and the discoloration extends proximally without blanching.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · ACROCYANOSIS | LIVEDO RETICULARIS | PERNIO (CHILBLAINS)

items.question: In primary livedo reticularis, what is a common characteristic of the mottling?
options: It worsens after exposure to warmth.
items.explanation: The mottling in primary livedo reticularis is more prominent after cold exposure and improves with warming.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · ACROCYANOSIS | LIVEDO RETICULARIS | PERNIO (CHILBLAINS)

items.question: Which condition associated with pernio involves intimal proliferation and perivascular infiltration of leukocytes?
items.explanation: Pathologic examination of pernio demonstrates angitis with intimal proliferation and perivascular infiltration.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · ACROCYANOSIS | LIVEDO RETICULARIS | PERNIO (CHILBLAINS)

items.subtopic: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities | PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)
items.statement: Peripheral artery disease primarily affects large and medium-size vessels.
items.explanation: Segmental lesions that cause stenosis or occlusion are usually localized to large and medium-size vessels.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Arterial Diseases of the Extremities | PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities | PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)
items.statement: Symptoms of peripheral artery disease are more common in upper extremities than lower extremities.
items.explanation: Symptoms are far more common in the lower than in the upper extremities.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Arterial Diseases of the Extremities | PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)

items.statement: Magnetic resonance angiography should be used for routine diagnostic testing of PAD.
items.explanation: MRA is not recommended for routine diagnostic testing but before potential revascularization.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 2)

items.statement: Approximately one-third to one-half of patients with symptomatic PAD have evidence of coronary artery disease.
items.explanation: One-third to one-half of patients with symptomatic PAD show evidence of CAD.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 2)

items.statement: Beta-adrenergic blockers worsen claudication and should be avoided in patients with PAD.
items.explanation: Beta-adrenergic blockers do not worsen claudication and may be used to treat hypertension, especially in patients with coexistent CAD.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Peripheral Artery Disease (part 1)

items.statement: Elastic support hose are recommended for improving blood flow to the skin in PAD.
items.explanation: Elastic support hose should be avoided, as they reduce blood flow to the skin.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Peripheral Artery Disease (part 1)

items.statement: Vasodilators such as prostaglandins are effective in relieving symptoms of chronic limb-threatening ischemia.
items.explanation: Vasodilators, including prostaglandins, do not relieve symptoms or prevent limb loss in chronic limb-threatening ischemia.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Peripheral Artery Disease (part 2) | REVASCULARIZATION

items.statement: Endovascular interventions for PAD include percutaneous transluminal balloon angioplasty and stent placement.
items.explanation: Endovascular interventions such as PTA and stent placement are used in treating peripheral artery disease.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Peripheral Artery Disease (part 2) | REVASCULARIZATION

items.statement: Thromboangiitis obliterans primarily affects proximal vessels.
items.explanation: Thromboangiitis obliterans primarily affects distal small and medium-size arteries and veins.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · FIBROMUSCULAR DYSPLASIA | THROMBOANGIITIS OBLITERANS

items.statement: Abstention from tobacco is a specific treatment for thromboangiitis obliterans.
items.explanation: The only specific treatment for thromboangiitis obliterans is abstention from tobacco.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · FIBROMUSCULAR DYSPLASIA | THROMBOANGIITIS OBLITERANS

items.statement: Arterial emboli tend to lodge at vessel bifurcations.
items.explanation: Emboli lodge at vessel bifurcations due to the decrease in vessel caliber.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · OTHER VASCULITIDES | ACUTE LIMB ISCHEMIA

items.statement: Trauma is a common cause of acute limb ischemia.
items.explanation: Trauma can disrupt blood flow and lead to acute arterial thrombosis.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · OTHER VASCULITIDES | ACUTE LIMB ISCHEMIA

items.statement: Immediate intervention to ensure reperfusion is not necessary in cases of severe ischemia.
items.explanation: Immediate intervention is indicated when limb viability is jeopardized by severe ischemia.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · CLINICAL FEATURES | Acute Limb Ischemia

items.statement: Long-term anticoagulation is recommended for acute limb ischemia caused by cardiac thromboembolism.
items.explanation: Long-term anticoagulation is indicated when the cause of ischemia is cardiac thromboembolism.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · CLINICAL FEATURES | Acute Limb Ischemia

items.statement: Atheroemboli typically lodge in large vessels, leading to absent distal pulses.
items.explanation: Atheroemboli tend to lodge in small vessels of the muscle and skin, so distal pulses usually remain palpable.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · ATHEROEMBOLISM | THORACIC OUTLET COMPRESSION SYNDROME

items.statement: Thoracic outlet compression syndrome can result from cervical ribs or abnormalities of the scalenus anticus muscle.
items.explanation: Compression at the thoracic outlet may be caused by cervical ribs or abnormalities of the scalenus anticus muscle.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · ATHEROEMBOLISM | THORACIC OUTLET COMPRESSION SYNDROME

items.subtopic: Arterial Thoracic Outlet Compression Syndrome | POPLITEAL ARTERY ENTRAPMENT
items.statement: A chest x-ray can indicate the presence of cervical ribs in patients with arterial thoracic outlet compression syndrome.
items.explanation: Chest x-ray is used to identify cervical ribs, which may contribute to arterial thoracic outlet compression syndrome.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Arterial Thoracic Outlet Compression Syndrome | POPLITEAL ARTERY ENTRAPMENT

items.subtopic: Arterial Thoracic Outlet Compression Syndrome | POPLITEAL ARTERY ENTRAPMENT
items.statement: Popliteal artery entrapment typically affects elderly individuals.
items.explanation: Popliteal artery entrapment typically affects young athletic men and women.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Arterial Thoracic Outlet Compression Syndrome | POPLITEAL ARTERY ENTRAPMENT

items.statement: Congenital arteriovenous fistulas are often associated with birthmarks.
items.explanation: Congenital arteriovenous fistulas may be associated with birthmarks.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · POPLITEAL ARTERY ANEURYSM | ARTERIOVENOUS FISTULA

items.statement: Acquired arteriovenous fistulas are typically difficult to treat surgically.
items.explanation: Acquired arteriovenous fistulas are usually amenable to surgical treatment.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · POPLITEAL ARTERY ANEURYSM | ARTERIOVENOUS FISTULA

items.statement: In primary Raynaud phenomenon, more than half of the affected individuals are men.
items.explanation: Women are affected about five times more often than men in primary Raynaud phenomenon.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · RAYNAUD PHENOMENON (part 1)

items.statement: Raynaud phenomenon is always associated with abnormal nailfold capillaroscopy findings.
items.explanation: In primary Raynaud phenomenon, nailfold capillaroscopy reveals normal superficial capillaries.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · RAYNAUD PHENOMENON (part 1)

items.statement: Raynaud phenomenon is often seen in individuals using vibrating hand tools.
items.explanation: Vibration from hand tools like chain saws and jackhammers can lead to Raynaud phenomenon.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · RAYNAUD PHENOMENON (part 2) | Raynaud Phenomenon

items.statement: Tobacco use is recommended for patients with Raynaud phenomenon.
items.explanation: Tobacco use is contraindicated in patients with Raynaud phenomenon.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · RAYNAUD PHENOMENON (part 2) | Raynaud Phenomenon

items.statement: In primary acrocyanosis, patients commonly experience pain, ulcers, and gangrene.
items.explanation: Patients with primary acrocyanosis are generally asymptomatic and do not develop pain, ulcers, or gangrene.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · ACROCYANOSIS | LIVEDO RETICULARIS | PERNIO (CHILBLAINS)

items.statement: Secondary livedo reticularis can be associated with antiphospholipid antibodies.
items.explanation: Secondary livedo reticularis may occur with conditions like antiphospholipid antibodies.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · ACROCYANOSIS | LIVEDO RETICULARIS | PERNIO (CHILBLAINS)

items.subtopic: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities | PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)
items.assertion: Intermittent claudication is relieved by rest.
items.reason: It occurs during exercise and is relieved by rest because it is due to inadequate blood flow during increased demand.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Arterial Diseases of the Extremities | PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)

items.subtopic: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities | PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)
items.assertion: Peripheral artery disease is more prevalent in individuals with higher socioeconomic status.
items.reason: PAD is associated with lower socioeconomic status.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Arterial Diseases of the Extremities | PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 1)

items.assertion: The prognosis for patients with PAD is worse if they continue smoking.
items.reason: Smoking exacerbates the progression of PAD and increases cardiovascular risk.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 2)

items.assertion: Duplex ultrasonography is used for routine diagnostic testing of PAD.
items.reason: It combines B-mode imaging and Doppler flow velocity waveform analysis to detect stenotic lesions.
items.explanation: The assertion is false, but the reason correctly describes duplex ultrasonography's use.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · PERIPHERAL ARTERY DISEASE (part 2)

items.assertion: Intensive glucose lowering reduces the risk of amputations in patients with diabetes mellitus.
items.reason: GLP-1 agonists and SGLT2 inhibitors have beneficial cardiovascular effects in patients with diabetes and PAD.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Peripheral Artery Disease (part 1)

items.assertion: Vasodilators have been proven effective in treating PAD.
items.reason: During exercise, peripheral vasodilation occurs distal to sites of significant arterial stenoses, reducing perfusion pressure.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; vasodilators have not proved beneficial for PAD.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Peripheral Artery Disease (part 1)

items.assertion: Preoperative cardiac risk assessment is crucial for patients undergoing revascularization.
items.reason: It identifies individuals at increased risk of adverse cardiac events during the perioperative period.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining why preoperative cardiac risk assessment is important.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Peripheral Artery Disease (part 2) | REVASCULARIZATION

items.assertion: Coronary angiography improves outcomes in most patients undergoing peripheral vascular surgery.
items.reason: Optimal medical therapy alone does not improve outcomes compared to coronary artery revascularization.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as coronary angiography and revascularization do not improve outcomes in most cases, but the reason is true.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · Peripheral Artery Disease (part 2) | REVASCULARIZATION

items.assertion: Acute limb ischemia severity depends on the occlusion location.
items.reason: The presence and development of collateral blood vessels affect ischemia severity.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · OTHER VASCULITIDES | ACUTE LIMB ISCHEMIA

items.assertion: Amputation is performed when the limb is not viable.
items.reason: Characterized by loss of sensation, paralysis, and absence of Doppler-detected blood flow in both arteries and veins.
items.explanation: Both assertion and reason are true; the reason correctly explains why amputation occurs when a limb is not viable.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · CLINICAL FEATURES | Acute Limb Ischemia

items.assertion: Raynaud phenomenon can be a presenting symptom of systemic sclerosis.
items.reason: It occurs in 80–90% of patients with systemic sclerosis and is the presenting symptom in 30%.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why Raynaud phenomenon can be a presenting symptom.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · RAYNAUD PHENOMENON (part 1)

items.assertion: Raynaud phenomenon in patients with primary pulmonary hypertension is coincidental.
items.reason: It may reflect a neurohumoral abnormality affecting both the pulmonary and digital circulations.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because Raynaud phenomenon's occurrence in primary pulmonary hypertension reflects an underlying neurohumoral abnormality.
items.reference: Arterial Diseases of the Extremities · RAYNAUD PHENOMENON (part 1)
